explanation: The > operator compares whether x is greater than y. Since 10 is NOT greater than 20, the expression evaluates to false. Comparison operators always return a boolean value (true or false).

The && (AND) operator returns true only when BOTH operands are true. Since b is false, the result is false. Think of it like needing BOTH keys to open a safe — if one key is missing, you can't open it.

Java evaluates if-else-if chains top to bottom and stops at the FIRST true condition. score >= 90 is false (85 < 90), so it moves on. score >= 80 is true (85 >= 80), so it prints "B" and skips the else. Even though 85 would also satisfy later conditions, only the first match executes.

question: What does the `!` operator do in Java?
options: Flips a boolean value (true becomes false, false becomes true)
Checks if a value is not null
explanation: The ! (NOT) operator is a boolean negation operator. It flips true to false and false to true. For example, !true is false and !false is true. It's like flipping a light switch.

These are two separate if statements (not if-else). Each one is evaluated independently. The first condition (age >= 16) is true, so "Can drive" prints. The second condition (age >= 18) is false, so "Can vote" does NOT print. If these were connected with else-if, only the first true one would run.

The || (OR) operator returns true if AT LEAST ONE operand is true. Since sunny is true, sunny || warm is true. The && (AND) operator returns true only if BOTH are true. Since warm is false, sunny && warm is false.

question: According to De Morgan's Laws, which expression is equivalent to `!(a && b)`?
explanation: De Morgan's Laws tell us how to distribute NOT over AND/OR. When you negate an AND, it becomes OR with each part negated: !(a && b) is the same as !a || !b. Similarly, !(a || b) becomes !a && !b. Think of it as: NOT flips BOTH the operator AND each operand.

When you use `new String()`, Java creates a brand new object in memory. The == operator compares memory addresses (are they the same object?), so s1 == s2 is false — they're different objects. The .equals() method compares content (do they contain the same text?), so s1.equals(s2) is true. ALWAYS use .equals() to compare Strings!

This uses nested conditionals. First, x > 3 is true (5 > 3), so we enter the outer if. Inside, x > 10 is false (5 is not > 10), so we go to the inner else and print "medium". The outer else ("small") is never reached because the outer if was true.

This demonstrates short-circuit evaluation. The && operator evaluates left to right and STOPS as soon as it finds a false. Since a != 0 is false, Java never evaluates (10 / a) > 1 — so the division by zero never happens! Without short-circuiting, this would crash. The else runs and prints "no".

First condition: temp > 80 && !raining → 75 > 80 is false. Since && needs both to be true, it's false (short-circuits). Second condition: temp > 60 || !raining → 75 > 60 is true. Since || only needs one to be true, it's true (short-circuits, doesn't check !raining). So "Park day!" prints.

The if-else-if chain stops at the first true condition. 72 >= 90? No. 72 >= 80? No. 72 >= 70? Yes! So result is "C" and the chain stops — it never checks >= 60. The second println is an independent statement: 72 >= 60 is true.

question: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(x >= 5 && x <= 10)`?
explanation: Using De Morgan's Law: !(A && B) = !A || !B. So !(x >= 5 && x <= 10) becomes !(x >= 5) || !(x <= 10), which simplifies to x < 5 || x > 10. This makes intuitive sense too: "NOT between 5 and 10" means "less than 5 OR greater than 10".

Breaking it down: x*x + y*y = 9 + 16 = 25. z*z = 25. So 25 == 25 is true (it's a 3-4-5 right triangle!). Then x + y > z → 3 + 4 > 5 → 7 > 5 is true. true && true = true. Both conditions of && are satisfied.

This is a classic safe null-check pattern using short-circuit evaluation. The && operator evaluates left to right. name != null is false (name IS null), so Java short-circuits and NEVER evaluates name.length() — avoiding the NullPointerException. The else block runs, printing "Invalid". Always check for null BEFORE calling methods!
